section.title: Eliphaz's Second Speech
section.sectionRationale: The second cycle of speeches begins, and Eliphaz has lost the gentleness of his first address. He now accuses Job of undermining the fear of God, appeals again to the wisdom of elders, and paints an extended, almost gleeful picture of the wicked man's inevitable terror and ruin, a portrait he clearly intends Job to recognize himself in.
section.thesis: Eliphaz's second speech sharpens his earlier principle into open accusation, charging Job with arrogance and irreverence, and devotes most of its length to a vivid description of the wicked man's collapse that functions as a thinly veiled portrait of what Eliphaz now believes Job to be.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Eliphaz's rebuke that Job's words are empty and undermine the fear of God; his challenge asking whether Job was present at creation or has access to secrets no one else has; his extended, foreboding description of the wicked man's terror and inescapable fear; and his closing portrait of the wicked man's total ruin, stretched out against God and doomed to barrenness.

units.label: You undermine the fear of God

verses.text: Then Eliphaz the Temanite replied:
"Does a wise man answer with empty counsel or fill his belly with the hot {{qadim|east wind}}?
Should he argue with useless words or speeches that serve no purpose?
But you even undermine the fear of God and hinder meditation before Him.
For your iniquity instructs your mouth, and you choose the language of the crafty.
Your own mouth, not mine, condemns you; your own lips testify against you.

units.label: Were you the first man ever born?
units.note: if even the heavens are not pure in His eyes

verses.text: "Were you the first man ever born? Were you brought forth before the hills?
Do you listen in on the {{sod|council}} of God or limit wisdom to yourself?
What do you know that we do not? What do you understand that is not clear to us?
Both the gray-haired and the aged are on our side, men much older than your father.
Are the consolations of God not enough for you, even words spoken gently to you?
Why has your heart carried you away, and why do your eyes flash,
as you turn your spirit against God and pour such words from your mouth?
What is man, that he should be pure, or one born of woman, that he should be righteous?
If God puts no trust in His holy ones, if even the heavens are not pure in His eyes,
how much less man, who is vile and corrupt, who drinks injustice like water?

units.label: The wicked man writhes in pain
units.note: he knows the day of darkness is at hand

verses.text: "Listen to me and I will inform you. I will describe what I have seen,
what was declared by wise men and was not concealed from their fathers,
to whom alone the land was given when no foreigner passed among them.
A {{rasha|wicked}} man writhes in pain all his days; only a few years are reserved for the ruthless.
Sounds of terror fill his ears; in his prosperity the destroyer attacks him.
He despairs of his return from darkness; he is marked for the sword.
He wanders about as food for vultures; he knows the day of darkness is at hand.
Distress and anguish terrify him, overwhelming him like a king poised to attack.

units.label: He stretched out his hand against God
units.note: their womb is pregnant with deceit

verses.text: "For he has stretched out his hand against God and has vaunted himself against the Almighty,
rushing headlong at Him with a thick, studded shield.
Though his face is covered with fat and his waistline bulges with flesh,
he will dwell in ruined cities, in abandoned houses destined to become rubble.
He will no longer be rich; his wealth will not endure. His possessions will not overspread the land.
He will not escape from the darkness; the flame will wither his shoots, and the breath of God's mouth will carry him away.
Let him not deceive himself with trust in emptiness, for emptiness will be his reward.
It will be paid in full before his time, and his branch will not flourish.
He will be like a vine stripped of its unripe grapes, like an olive tree that sheds its blossoms.
For the company of the godless will be barren, and fire will consume the tents of bribery.
They conceive trouble and give birth to evil; their womb is pregnant with {{mirmah|deceit}}."

qadim.gloss: The east wind, a hot, destructive desert wind in the ancient Near East, often associated in Scripture with judgment and withering. Eliphaz accuses Job of filling his belly with such wind, empty, damaging speech rather than substance.

sod.gloss: A council, intimate circle, or secret counsel. Eliphaz mockingly asks whether Job has listened in on God's own council, the same word used positively elsewhere of the LORD's confiding His counsel with those who fear Him, Psalm 25:14, turned here into an accusation of presumption.

rasha.gloss: The wicked, guilty, or condemned one, a central category in wisdom literature contrasted with the righteous, tsaddiq. Eliphaz's extended portrait of the rasha's inevitable terror and collapse functions as a barely disguised warning to Job, implying he now belongs to this category.

mirmah.gloss: Deceit, treachery, fraud. Eliphaz closes his speech by describing the wicked as a womb pregnant with mirmah, a vivid image of evil conceived and inevitably born, meant to warn Job of the fruit his own supposed inner corruption must eventually yield.

basic.context: Eliphaz speaks again, now more sharply than in his first address, accusing Job of undermining reverence for God with empty words drawn from his own iniquity. He challenges Job's presumption, asking whether he was present at creation or has secret access to God's council that other, wiser and older men lack. He then devotes most of his speech to an extended, almost relished description of the wicked man's inevitable terror, poverty, and ruin, an image clearly meant to warn Job that this fate awaits him unless he repents.
basic.hermeneutics: This speech shows the friends' theology hardening from gentle suggestion into direct accusation across the second cycle. Eliphaz's description of the wicked contains real and vivid poetic power, and portions of it echo true patterns Scripture elsewhere affirms about persistent, unrepentant wickedness; the error, again, is the specific and unevidenced application to Job, whom the reader knows to be innocent of the sins this portrait implies.
basic.originalLanguages: Eliphaz accuses Job of filling his belly with qadim, the hot east wind, empty and destructive speech, and mocks him for claiming access to God's sod, council, that wiser men lack. His extended portrait of the rasha, the wicked man, closes with the image of a womb pregnant with mirmah, deceit, evil conceived and inevitably born, aimed pointedly at Job though never naming him outright.
basic.typology: Eliphaz's insistence that no one, not even the heavens, is pure in God's eyes rightly anticipates the full biblical doctrine of universal sinfulness, though his application of it to explain Job's specific suffering remains mistaken; the tension between a true general doctrine and its wrongful specific application recurs throughout this cycle of speeches.
basic.christ: Eliphaz's claim that even the heavens are not pure in God's eyes, and how much less man, rightly anticipates Paul's later verdict that none is righteous, answered fully only in Christ, whose righteousness is given to sinners rather than achieved by them. Where Eliphaz uses universal sinfulness to accuse a specific innocent sufferer, the gospel uses the same universal truth to invite every sinner to the same free mercy, without requiring them to first prove which specific sin caused their present trouble.
basic.god: God is portrayed as a searching, exacting judge before whom even angels and heavens fall short, a true picture as far as it goes. But the picture becomes distorted when Eliphaz assumes this exacting God must presently be punishing Job for some particular, locatable fault, rather than allowing that God's dealings with the righteous can include suffering with no such explanation.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 3:10-12 affirms no one is righteous, none who does good, echoing Eliphaz's claim about universal impurity; Psalm 25:14 speaks of the LORD's council, sod, shared with those who fear Him, a positive use of the same word Eliphaz uses accusingly; Proverbs 16:18 warns that pride precedes destruction, a theme underlying Eliphaz's portrait of the wicked man's downfall.
basic.oneStory: Eliphaz's second speech hardens true doctrine into a weaponized accusation against an innocent man, showing how quickly right theology can curdle into cruelty when detached from actual knowledge of a person's situation. His true insistence on universal human impurity finds its truly gracious answer not in accusation but in the righteousness freely given in Christ.

context.literary: This speech opens the second of three cycles, and its harsher tone compared to Eliphaz's first address shows the friends' patience wearing thin as Job continues to resist their diagnosis of his suffering.
context.historical: The east wind, qadim, was a well-known destructive force in the ancient Near East, a hot, dry wind from the desert associated with withering crops and divine judgment, making Eliphaz's insult about Job's words especially vivid to his original hearers.
context.canonical: Eliphaz's claim that not even the heavens are pure in God's sight, verse 15, anticipates later biblical affirmations of universal sinfulness, showing that even flawed messengers in Scripture can voice theologically accurate statements the rest of the canon will confirm.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the friends' theology hardening in its second cycle from gentle suggestion into open, extended accusation, using a portrait of the wicked man's downfall as a barely veiled warning to an innocent sufferer.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter accurately describes Eliphaz's escalating accusation; it does not prescribe this kind of extended, thinly veiled warning as a model for addressing someone in pain. What remains as legitimate doctrine is Eliphaz's underlying claim about universal human sinfulness, which Scripture elsewhere confirms, even as his specific application to Job's suffering is exposed as false by the book's own frame.

debates.question: Why does Eliphaz's portrait of the wicked man (15:20-35) grow so extensive and vivid?

views.label: Sincere pastoral warning meant to frighten Job into repentance
views.case: Eliphaz may genuinely believe he is doing Job a kindness by painting so vividly what awaits the unrepentant, hoping fear will move Job to seek God's mercy before it is too late.

views.label: An accusation disguised as general teaching, avoiding direct confrontation while still condemning Job
views.case: By never naming Job directly yet clearly intending the portrait to apply to him, Eliphaz can accuse without appearing to accuse, a rhetorical strategy that increases rather than decreases the speech's cruelty.

debates.bearing: However Eliphaz's motive is judged, the extended length and vividness of this portrait, compared to his shorter first speech, signals the friends' patience wearing out and their theology hardening into harsher applications as the cycles of speeches continue.

study.originalLanguages: Eliphaz accuses Job of empty speech, qadim, the hot east wind, and mocks his claim to special access to God's sod, council. His extended portrait of the rasha, wicked man, closes with a womb pregnant with mirmah, deceit, a vivid image of evil's inevitable fruit, aimed at Job though carefully left unnamed.

typology.type: The claim that not even the heavens are pure in God's eyes, so how much less man
typology.fulfillment: Romans 3:10-12's full doctrine of universal sinfulness, answered by the righteousness of Christ given to all who believe.

typology.type: The wicked man's stretched-out hand against God and inevitable ruin
typology.fulfillment: The final judgment of the unrepentant, contrasted with the mercy freely offered in Christ to all who turn to Him, wicked and righteous alike.

study.christ: Eliphaz rightly says not even the heavens are pure before God, and how much less man; Paul will confirm this exact truth in Romans 3, that none is righteous, not one. But where Eliphaz uses this truth to condemn Job specifically without evidence, the gospel uses this same universal truth to level the ground beneath every sinner and offer, without exception, the righteousness of Christ to anyone who will receive it, whether their suffering has an identifiable cause or not.
study.god: God is portrayed here as an exacting judge before whom even His holy ones and the heavens themselves fall short, a true and weighty picture. The danger the chapter exposes is the leap from this true general doctrine to a false specific verdict, assuming God's exacting justice must presently be punishing Job for a particular sin only Eliphaz claims to discern.

crossReferences.note: None is righteous, no not one, echoing and confirming Eliphaz's claim about universal human impurity before God.
The LORD's council, sod, is with those who fear Him, a positive use of the same word Eliphaz uses accusingly against Job in 15:8.
Pride goes before destruction, a theme resonant with Eliphaz's portrait of the wicked man's collapse after vaunting himself against the Almighty.

study.oneStory: Eliphaz's second speech hardens a true doctrine, universal human sinfulness, into a cruel, extended accusation against a man the reader knows to be innocent of the specific charge implied. The gospel takes that same true doctrine and turns it toward grace rather than condemnation, offering Christ's righteousness freely to sinners rather than demanding they first locate their own hidden fault.

quiz.q: What accusation does Eliphaz make against Job in 15:4-6 that is sharper than his first speech?

options.text: That Job undermines the fear of God and that his own words condemn him, not Eliphaz's accusations.
options.note: This direct charge of irreverence marks an escalation from the gentler tone of Eliphaz's first speech in chapters 4 to 5.

options.text: That Job has stolen from the poor.
options.note: No such specific charge of theft appears in this speech; the accusation concerns Job's irreverent speech, not a named crime.

options.text: That Job's friends have all abandoned him.
options.note: Eliphaz is not describing the friends' own actions but accusing Job directly of undermining reverence for God.

options.text: That Job has never actually suffered any real loss.
options.note: Eliphaz does not deny Job's suffering; his accusation concerns the content and attitude of Job's speech.

quiz.q: What challenge does Eliphaz pose to Job in 15:7-10?

options.text: Whether Job was present at creation or has access to God's secret council that wiser, older men do not have.
options.note: Eliphaz mocks Job's presumption in claiming insight the collective wisdom of elders supposedly does not possess.

options.text: Whether Job has ever actually prayed to God.
options.note: The challenge concerns claimed special knowledge, not whether Job prays at all.

options.text: Whether Job's wealth was ever legitimately earned.
options.note: This is not the subject of Eliphaz's challenge here; his focus is on claimed wisdom and insight.

options.text: Whether Job's friends are older than he is.
options.note: Eliphaz does mention that older, wiser men agree with the friends' position, but his main challenge concerns Job's presumed access to divine secrets, not simple age comparison.

quiz.q: What does most of Eliphaz's second speech (15:17-35) describe?

options.text: An extended, vivid portrait of the wicked man's inevitable terror, poverty, and ruin.
options.note: This portrait occupies most of the chapter and is clearly intended as a veiled warning to Job.

options.text: A detailed defense of Job's innocence.
options.note: The chapter argues the opposite; Eliphaz's portrait implies Job belongs among the wicked he describes.

options.text: Practical instructions for offering sacrifices.
options.note: No sacrificial instructions appear in this chapter; its content is a poetic description of the wicked's downfall.

options.text: A neutral historical account with no application to Job.
options.note: Though Job is never named directly in this portrait, its placement and rhetorical function clearly aim it at him.

quiz.q: What true doctrine does Eliphaz correctly affirm in 15:14-16, even while misapplying his overall argument?

options.text: That no human being, and not even the heavens, is pure before God.
options.note: This claim of universal impurity before God is affirmed elsewhere in Scripture, such as Romans 3:10-12, even though Eliphaz misuses it to accuse Job specifically.

options.text: That some people are entirely without sin.
options.note: Eliphaz explicitly denies this, insisting no one, not even the heavens, is pure in God's eyes.

options.text: That God's council is available to anyone who asks.
options.note: Eliphaz uses the idea of God's council, sod, to mock Job's presumed access to it, not to offer it broadly.

options.text: That wickedness always brings immediate wealth.
options.note: Eliphaz's portrait actually describes the wicked man's wealth as fleeting and ultimately destroyed, not secured.

quiz.q: How should readers evaluate the mixture of true doctrine and false application in this chapter?

options.text: By recognizing that Eliphaz's general claims about human sinfulness are true, while his specific application of them to accuse Job is false, since the reader already knows Job's suffering was not caused by hidden sin.
options.note: This distinction between sound general doctrine and its unproven specific application is central to understanding all three friends' speeches.

options.text: By concluding that everything Eliphaz says in this chapter must be rejected as false.
options.note: Some of Eliphaz's claims, such as universal human sinfulness, are affirmed elsewhere in Scripture as true; the error is specifically in his application to Job.

options.text: By concluding that Job must indeed be guilty as Eliphaz suggests.
options.note: The book's own frame in chapters 1 and 2 establishes that Job's suffering was not caused by sin, directly contradicting Eliphaz's application.

options.text: By ignoring the chapter entirely as having no theological value.
options.note: The chapter has real theological value both in its accurate general claims and as a cautionary example of misapplying true doctrine.

questions: Eliphaz's warnings about the wicked man's downfall are vivid, detailed, and aimed at someone he never actually names. Have you ever spoken indirectly about someone's supposed faults rather than addressing them honestly and directly?

Eliphaz correctly says no one is pure before God, yet uses that truth cruelly. What true things about human sinfulness do you know, and how do you make sure you use them to humble yourself rather than to judge others?

Eliphaz assumes that because Job disagrees with the collective wisdom of elders, he must be wrong. Where do you weigh popular or traditional opinion against your own honest reading of a situation, and how do you tell the difference between humility and mere conformity?

This speech shows theology hardening into accusation across repeated cycles of conflict. Have you noticed your own position toward someone hardening over the course of a disagreement, even when the underlying facts have not changed?

Eliphaz's true doctrine of universal sinfulness is meant by Scripture to lead to grace in Christ, not condemnation of a specific sufferer. Where do you need to receive that grace for yourself this week rather than turning true doctrine into self-accusation or the judgment of others?

translationNotes.note: The hot east wind, qadim, was a well-recognized destructive force in the ancient Near East, often linked to divine judgment elsewhere in Scripture, such as the wind that dries up Jonah's plant, Jonah 4:8.

Council renders sod, elsewhere used positively of the LORD's intimate counsel shared with those who fear Him, Psalm 25:14; Eliphaz here uses the same word mockingly to accuse Job of claiming an access he does not have.

Their womb is pregnant with deceit is a vivid metaphor of evil conceived internally and inevitably brought to birth, a common wisdom-literature image for how sin produces its own destructive consequences.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Job 15:1-35. The em dash in verse 10 of the source text was replaced with a comma per house style; no other substantive changes were made to the verse text.
